column1:
Do you think the ideas generated by the AI are useful to you? Why or why not?
For the original prompt, most of the ideas generated by the AI were of notably limited value to the main aim. Among the 13 provided points, #1-4 and #6-11 are things that an AI program can be directly used to do, hence they will likely not be good checks to catch AI usage.

#5 (Draft Workshops) may be useful, depending if I hold the workshop during class/in person and if I add an additional requirement that students must verbally explain core aspects of their papers to peers. Furthermore, to give UTVerse some credit here, the final proposal about peer interaction is a useful idea. Similarly, #12 (Oral Presentation) could guarantee that at least the act of presenting was not AI generated, although it cannot guarantee that the script was made without assistance of a generative AI.

#13 (Penalties for Non-compliance) was the most useful from my view and, in turn, was the core of my modified prompt. The email generated from the modified prompt could be of future use, although it needs plenty more work before serving as a standard email template.  

How effectively do the AI's suggestions address your needs or learning outcomes?
For the original prompt, the AI's suggestions were not very effective. However, I was not surprised by the lack of effectiveness for two main reasons. First, while I aimed to include the four basic elements of a prompt, my submitted original prompt was intentionally sparse in detail.  I made this choice to see how much depth could be built into assignment instructions from a rather open-ended starting point. Second, while UTVerse has a robust knowledge source, my prompt was fairly reflexive. I wouldn't expect a generative AI program to answer a reflexive prompt with ease, especially for the first query in a conversation.

What problems or shortcomings do you notice in the AI's responses?
One major shortcoming for both the original and modified prompt was that the AI did not explain things in much detail. For instance, while UTVerse successfully synthesized information from several sources, including searching the attached pdf, the AI did not provide the student with more than a surface level reasoning concerning what was wrong about academic dishonesty in this case. Part of this limitation is likely my prompt engineering, although another part could be that the AI lacks relevant background information on the wrongs and worries about academic dishonesty, which would make it impossible to convey that message in more than a superficial manner.

Which AI-generated ideas do you believe could be most effectively incorporated into your course or research? How would you implement these ideas?
The most effective incorporation for the ideas generated from the original prompt is to build a major iterative assignment for my WC courses, including parts that are in-person and involve active, immediate, non-written communication between student peers. I can also take the original prompt response from UTVerse and, through applying different modified prompts and prompt templates, see if it can generate a specific set of detailed instructions for an iterative final essay assignment.

Exploring the ideas generated by the AI left me with a mix of curiosity and critical engagement. Some of the AI's suggestions, particularly around cultural identity exploration and role-play simulations for multicultural negotiation, offer a helpful structure from which to start. These ideas resonate with my broader goals of building a Hebrew-speaking community and fostering intercultural empathy. However, I also noticed a recurring need for more cultural specificity, depth, and emotional nuance. The responses often miss the rich, complex layers that define Israeli society and its diversity, making them sometimes detached from lived experiences.

While the AI's effectiveness is evident in providing broad frameworks and engaging prompts, its real value lies in its ability to support, not replace, human facilitation and cultural context. I see potential in incorporating AI-generated cultural reflection activities and negotiation simulations, but they require thoughtful adaptation. Integrating real-world examples, incorporating authentic perspectives from diverse Israeli communities, and using tailored discussions would ensure these activities maintain their intended depth and impact.

I plan to use the AI-generated case study and discussion questions in the PUBH 489 internship class meeting this Thursday. I struggle with writing concise case study scenarios, so the AI tool was a time saver. Overall, I am pleased with the scenario, but I will make additional edits before using it in class. For example, I want to add in some details from my own professional work experiences to make it more real-world. I am pleased with the political overlay that AI added with my second prompt. One limitation was the UT Verse timed out and ended mid-sentence in the second output though I was able to prompt it to keep going. 

As for integrating AI tools into my teaching, this semester I started an open AI use course policy and have encouraged and demonstrated UT Verse for in-class activities and class assignments. In the spring, I would like to add an early discussion of why I am encouraging AI use in order to get buy-in and a shared understanding. I also want to adapt the in-class AI activities for online students.

1. Reflect on the process and outcomes in 1-2 paragraphs. Consider the following questions:

Do you think the ideas generated by the AI are useful to you? Why or why not?

As long as I use them as thought-starters, then I think they are very useful. I'm not sure UT Verse is the platform for me yet, but hopefully it will be soon. I'd like to be able to upload the resume template into UT Verse so students can generate their output using the exact design that my assignment requires. This way they can focus on the two main things, writing bullet points as accomplishments and integrity/honesty. 

2. How effectively do the AI's suggestions address your needs or learning outcomes?

I didn't give the AI my specific learning outcomes, and I will try this activity again in my team version of ChatGPT 4.o and see kind of results it gives me.

3. What problems or shortcomings do you notice in the AI's responses?

When I said online class, I didn't specify "asynchronous" and the AI didn't ask me to clarify, it just gave me activities for face-to-face learning. It would be most beneficial if it asked me to clarify the things that I miss, instead of giving me whatever it has (but a simple browser search does the same thing).

4. Which AI-generated ideas do you believe could be most effectively incorporated into your course or research? How would you implement these ideas?

I'm not using AI for my research because I don't want to unintentionally violate any ethical standards for original work. I love AI-generated ideas for class activities, but they are already getting repetitive, and they need to expand to keep up. I'm not going near AI with my research until after I finish my dissertation next semester. 

I would love to have some resources that you all trust and believe that are specific to using GenAI in research.

Do you think the ideas generated by the AI are useful to you? Why or why not?  RE)  It looks helpful. It generates what I have wanted.
How effectively do the AI's suggestions address your needs or learning outcomes?  RE) It is very close to what I have expected.
What problems or shortcomings do you notice in the AI's responses?   RE) Need to double check tis accuracy and validity. Some points need to be in more details.
Which AI-generated ideas do you believe could be most effectively incorporated into your course or research? How would you implement these ideas?   RE) GIS Mapping of Global Freshwater Distribution. Need to get prepared for the software, etc.

a. The ideas generated by the AI were very useful, coherent, and provided the latest proven resources.

b. They came very close to what I needed. The class I used them for will have five modules; I limited my questions to the first module. I plan to repeat and refine the process by requesting ideas for the complete course.

c. The responses were very broad. Initially, some of the given links were broken, but after requesting, I received working links.

d. I need to dig deeper to adequately respond to the questions posed.

The activities generated by AI were useful because they were engaging and appropriate for the Spanish 111-112 student. The suggestions encourage students  to introduce themselves, greet, and ask others about basic, personal information. Asking and answering these questions through the use of cards and bingo really address the learning outcomes in an interactive and fun way. I have not noticed any shortcomings in this specific AI response. I believe I can successfully apply the suggested activities in my first day of class.

I think that the assignment crafted (prompt 1) works better than the sample completed assignment (prompt 2). However, I can definitely modify the sample completed assignment. I can either do this without using GenAI or I can add more information into my AI prompt. Mainly, I want to incorporate some elements of the class readings on crafting a research question. I think I would like to use Booth et al Craft of Research’s ‘three-step’ model for this. I would, though, first have to provide the GenAI with that model. I would also edit the paragraph-length directions in the assignment itself (prompt 1) to sound more like me. This was an interesting experiment and I’m curious to see how I can improve my prompt writing.

The suggestions effectively address my needs by providing a variety of interactive and culturally relevant activities, which can enhance student participation and understanding. They offer practical strategies that can be easily incorporated into my teaching practice. However, some ideas might need simplification or adaptation to fit the specific context and time constraints of my course. The most effective ideas include quizzes and polls, which I have already implemented through platforms like Kahoot and during Zoom classes, respectively. These tools have proven to be highly engaging and beneficial for reinforcing concepts. Additionally, the incorporation of culturally relevant content helps to make the learning experience more meaningful and relatable for students.

I thought this was a fun activity! My results were very useful as a springboard for ideas for a real lesson. Two things I really, really struggle with in my professional writing course are: 1) having enough good examples of different genres of real workplace writing for use to analyze and discuss and 2) coming up with realistic scenarios or role-playing activities that encourage students to think through different roles in a wide variety of professions. So, I plan on using heavily-edited AI-generated samples quite a bit this semester to prepare some professional documents and examples of workplace communication that we can discuss.

The results here were very useful for providing an activity idea and structuring a lesson with competing goals—in this case, teaching the concept of genre while also relating ideas about genre to workplace personalities, relationship dynamics, and constraints, which each influence how rhetorically successful one genre of document might be over another. The biggest problem I noticed in the first result was that there wasn’t much emphasis on the concept of genre throughout the lesson (the first result focused mostly on different scenarios of workplace communication), but the revised version left space for a discussion about how choosing the most appropriate genre for internal communication has ethical implications. I think the activity would need to be adapted to have students draft their responses to their scenarios together, where I’d also need to provide a specific genre (memo, email, phone call/voicemail, work chat, etc), and then have students reflect on whether or not that was the most rhetorically effective genre to use and its ethical implications. (For example, I could pair the “Responding With Empathy” scenario with the genre of instant message/work chat, which poses an ethical problem if the work chat is public to the entire department.

One thing I’m thinking about: AI lesson planning might help us see through some of the complexities of the “curse of knowledge” (aka, when instructors forget what it’s like to not know something and thus don’t have the tools to explain the concept to beginners). So, I think AI has the potential to help instructors structure lessons by helping us step outside of our expertise to remember what it’s like to not know something. I might think that the first place to start in a lesson like this is on genre analysis rather than the basics of different genres of internal communication, so it could help us see different routes into more effective lesson scopes.

I believe that the AI's lesson plan for teaching "por" and "para" in SPAN 211 is valuable because it incorporates diverse activities such as visual aids, interactive exercises, group discussions, and multimedia elements. These methods address different learning styles and help students effectively understand and use these prepositions. The plan aligns well with the course goals by balancing theoretical knowledge and practical application, thereby enhancing language skills. However, it presumes the availability of resources like projectors and sufficient time for group activities.

I was very impressed with the AI responses (ChatGPT) and definitively worth of considering when planning a class. The requested report illustrates in a very concise manner a complex topic for Spanish students. I also find very practical and straight forward the suggestions for introducing the topic in class. In general, I think the response provided by the AI fulfills my goal of introducing in a practical and effective manner a grammatical idea that students typically struggle with along the semester. The only part that I don't find convincing is the proposed communicative activity. I think the instructions are not clear and lacks the communicative engagement that I was looking for. However, I'm sure the prompt for the activity could be refined for a better outcome.

Before inputing my prompts, I had expectations (from the module) that these tools might really be able to create course material (for better or worse). After experimenting with the prompts, I feel better about my potential as a faculty member to create added value for my courses that can not yet be replaced fully by a bot. Copilot is interesting, and I'm excited to continue to play with it, but I don't know if I will use it to create or guide my course material, particularly for a course I've already taught once before. I hoped the bot might be more creative or even step in and offer content that might challenge my own biases. 

The biggest shortcoming I see with these bots is the shortcoming I saw in student essays that used AI: the content is not that specific, personal or creative. I wonder if my bot will continue to learn, and how fast it will learn, if I start using it for daily tasks, but right now it is not creating material that is super innovative or engaging. I'm excited to read some of the other responses in this discussion post though to see if AI might be better suited to other course subjects. I'm not anti-AI in any way — in fact I've really enjoyed this course and want to learn more — but my initial experiments with Copilot did not blow me away. 

In order to really test the potential of these bots and explore potentially more effective uses for them, I'd be curious to plug my entire syllabus into UTVerse and see if it can create content based on my learning outcomes in the course, but, overall, I'd give Copilot a 4/10 for its ability to address my needs (and Copilot was the most impressive of the resources I tried, at least with my first prompt). 

One of the ways I do plan to hopefully integrate AI into my course is to have the students do an activity similar to this. I love the AI literacy that is gained through this activity, and it's perfect for my introduction to journalism course!

I thought this was pretty good, but there are more directly applicable archives and hosted sites that relate to rock.

Overall, I thought the ideas generated were good, especially if I were a brand new instructor, and it pulled from a variety of not only activities but sources. I did think it missed the mark a little when asking for a .edu source for my site specifically, but with a little more work on the prompt or work on my own the assignment is basically done. 

I wonder what the applications are for generating literate reviews or quick bibliographies. However, I do fear that since it is "trained" with input from users, it will ultimately use the most popular info (like Google) and leave more niche sources and research further and further behind.

Overall, I was also a bit skeptical of using AI to generate ideas for course assignments and discussions, as I have also seen how it can be poorly applied by students in essays. The exercise was very eye opening for me in the way it can provide unique ways to engage students on the more difficult concepts or principles in a course. I chose to use UTVerse for this exercise. My first impression of this tool was it generated the basic traditional ideas for a typical assignment on Natural Selection in an ANTH 110 course. However, I specifically asked it for content that would challenge the Honors students that I teach. For example, it suggested the classic “Peppered Moth” Case study, which is a great example to illustrate the concept of natural selection, but for Honor’s students, likely would not be challenging enough. Therefore, I didn’t find it helpful in generating more advanced assignments and content for my attended student audience.  

I revised my question to direct it to more advanced examples that would help students connect the material to applied areas, like health. However, I was a bit disappointed with the results.  I asked it to generate an example/case study of natural selection, as it applies to medical sciences or public health. It is these case studies that can be more challenging for students to conceptualize and explain this type of phenomenon. However, I felt a bit disappointed in the results. In my opinion, it missed the mark on some of the case study suggestions, as there are many diseases that would have made great case studies examples, including sickle cell anemia. So, I am a bit torn as well on the use of this to create higher level content for advanced students. Therefore, I do not think this effectively helped me in meeting my learning outcomes because the examples provided were a bit simplistic for the audience I am trying to reach and EXPAND their knowledge of the content. I found the examples suggested were more repetitive of content in their text, but not expanding the breadth of their knowledge. However, it did provide some interesting discussion questions that I could consider working with or revising a bit to foster further discussion on this topic. I did like the homework or short reflexive assignment suggestions. So, it did provide some utility in thinking about other ways to foster a bit more advanced discussion on this topic or have the students engage with it further. I could see myself implementing more of the discussion questions and the potential homework suggestions into a small assignment.

Overall, I was not as impressed with UTVerse, as I had initially thought I would be. So, I did try to run my prompt through CoPilot to see if I would get a different result. I was impressed with this tool more, as it provided key terms, like selective selection, that did not emerge in any of my results with UTVerse. All key terms from CoPilot were also defined, without me having to directly specify that in my prompt. I think this is a big advantage for choosing CoPilot, as it seemed like it didn’t need as much direction as UTVerse required. The examples from CoPilot were not to the degree I was looking for either, as it also gave the same “Peppered Moth Case Study.” CoPilot also did not provide any short essay or homework suggestions, like UTVerse did with my original prompt. Obviously, it likely would have, if I had put that in my revision. However, it did provide ideas for a small group activity and discussion questions which seemed a bit better than what UTVerse had suggested. Overall, I noticed there were suttle differences between the two using the exact same prompt and exact same revision, which was interesting to me. In the future, I could see myself using CoPilot more, because it produced better key terms, definitions, and content. If I were to use it in my courses, it likely would only be to generate ideas for small activities, in class discussions, or reflexive assignments.

By way of background, I'm writing a proposal to a foundation.  It's a weird proposal in which you simply answer a lot of questions with word limits for each answer.  One question is, "During the Implementation Phase, JFF will offer optional Tech Pilots to help Anchor Partners and their Regional Teams accelerate the training and placement of workers in quality green jobs. In addition to the job training services or programs noted above, what (if any) technology-enabled solution(s) is your Regional Team interested in exploring, testing, or implementing via a JFF Tech Pilot? (150 words max)."  I wanted to see what AI might come up with as an answer to this question and to see how close it came to describing what my team already has in mind.  

Interestingly, while I am not that pleased with either response from Copilot, I found that UTVerse did an excellent job with the original prompt:

Imagine a centralized digital platform, "East Tennessee Works," tailored for seamless collaboration among all stakeholders in the labor market. This cutting-edge ecosystem features real-time job matching, skills assessment tools, and personalized training pathways to bridge the gap between job seekers and employers in advanced manufacturing and green construction industries. The platform fosters direct communication between unions, educational institutions, and community organizations, ensuring an efficient flow of information and resources to train and place individuals effectively. With robust analytics capabilities, it tracks progress toward our ambitious goal of empowering 5,000 people with sustainable careers, offering insights that drive continuous improvement in training programs and job placement strategies. "East Tennessee Works" isn't just a digital solution—it's the cornerstone of a community-wide initiative to revolutionize the local workforce landscape.

With some minor modifications, I could use the UTVerse response in my grant application.   One thing that I found alarming in the initial response from Copilot is that they described the federal government's SkillBridge program but made it sound original.  Copilot also assumed that people seeking work would directly interface with the system; UTVerse did not make this assumption.  When I clarified the prompt to indicate that I wanted something original and that I didn't want job seekers directly interfacing with the system, Copilot weirdly added that the system would create training modules.

Considering UT Verse's responses to my prompt, I think it provides a helpful starting point with creating a course like mine. The initial responses and topic areas given showed a good understanding of business-centric values that I could build on. When I sought more refined ideas regarding the synchronous and asynchronous aspects of the course, the ideas that were generated were pretty basic, particularly with the asynchronous ideas. One of the challenges I have with a GPT is that I have to type everything into the system and read the response, which limits the flow that would come from having a verbal conversation with a knowledgeable SME or colleague. There can be a lot of overhead time spent explaining the background and getting on the same page, and I have found that the GPT doesn't always accurately "remember" the details of what we previously talked about. Since GenAI isn't usually going to admit not knowing something or not having all of the information, I really like the idea of adding a line of 'Ask me any clarifying questions you need to make a detailed response' after the initial prompt. I didn't do that here, but have started doing that elsewhere. In this case, it assumed when I said it was an online class that everything related to being online (virtual volunteering, researching online businesses).

Regarding what could be incorporated into my class, I think the first responses were more useful than the second. They give a general overview of ideas and topics to hit on without getting into the weeds, which avoids detailed responses where the AI doesn't know the full picture. I like the idea of encouraging innovation and entrepreneurial thinking regarding community engagement. This could come from conversations where students identify community needs and share ways they individually and in groups can address these needs using their skills as businesspeople.

I am interested in adding a brief pre-check assessment to the course. This would be to gauge students' foreknowledge of the subject matter, their academic strengths and areas that need improvement, and their learning style. I want to start including group work to further help with engagement (particularly student-student and student-material), but do not want to randomly assign them and hope for the best. I would rather take into account how they learn and what their familiarity with the subject to better inform the groups. The quiz would have the added benefit to inform how I design and deliver the course in terms of having a good mix of written and audiovisual material for different learning styles as well as to have different levels of instruction for those that have a good foundation within sociology and for those that perhaps this is the first sociology course they have taken.

I think the quiz questions are mostly useful to me because they at worst give me a starting point and at best ask exactly what I need to know. The first response was a good start, but it also made evident the revisions to the prompt that I needed to make. I want to know students' foreknowledge of social inequalities, but to assume students know sociological terminology to begin with would assume a level of knowledge and I did not want to do that, so I let Gemini know not to assume any knowledge of social inequalities. I also noticed that it did not suggest being able to answer multiple ways, but I know that sometimes students learn in multiple ways and prefer a mix of instruction, therefore I asked Gemini to allow for multiple responses. Lastly, Gemini assumed I wanted to know strengths and weaknesses based on social science classes, but this may be a student's first social science class, so I need to know what a student's general academic strengths and weaknesses are. That informed my last revision. 

Gemini makes assumptions based on the terms you use within the entire prompt to inform specific choices it makes in its suggestions. Just because this is a sociology course, it assumed I only wanted answers based on a social science course and assumed some basic knowledge for the students, but that is what the quiz needs to gauge in the first place. I think asking GenAI specific prompts with specific requirements is best, but it also narrows the field a bit too much. The last quiz questions that it provided will be what I most likely base the quiz that I plan on implementing in the next iteration of this course. Because I teach online asynchronously, I may ask for further revisions to see how Gemini incorporates the teaching modality to the questions.

Honestly, this exercise should not provide the answers in the parenthesis. It seems to me that I should have stopped while i was ahead. The first output was great, but the others were full of fluf ..lol.  This practice makes me want to use AI even less than when I started. Obviously, Gen AI in Italian needs improvement. After all it is probably not its native language. LOL

ChatGPT was able to provide a quick fact-pattern for practice purposes, which is useful. One shortcoming is the relative simplicity of the original response - all the information was essential and stripped down, which is rarely the situation in real-life reporting. When it was asked in the follow-up prompt to add some ambiguity, it did a pretty good job of generating new quotes and information that muddy the waters for the student. 
This would possibly be a good use of AI in our media-writing course, but alongside a discussion about ethics in reporting - obviously, quotes and fact-patterns can't be made up.

I selected UT Verse to become more familiar with this tool for my courses. I was amazed at the speed at which UT Verse generated the case study from the prompt. Only seconds to have this information! This is definitely a tool that I can use in my courses. My students really enjoy case studies to help them learn content and apply that knowledge in a patient care scenario. The original case study came up with the clinical scenario of complaint of dizziness, fatigue, and occasional confusion that have worsened over the past few weeks. In my modified prompt, I added the new diagnosis of atrial fibrillation that would be consistent with the symptoms in the clinical scenario. The questions for students are right on for the critical thinking that students would need to apply pharmacology knowledge in this scenario. I can see very easily from this trial run for me in use of UT Verse that this would be a very helpful tool to develop an evolving case study where students would have to respond to a change in patient status from medication(s) that have been prescribed.

I feel the lesson plan that UTVerse generated would be very helpful in the classroom, in particular the portions that call for student group interaction and presentations. And the pros and cons it suggested in the lesson plan are all things that I have previously discussed in class.

As I mentioned above, I was surprised by the results I got, which were a lot better and more specific than I was expecting.  I’ve never assigned this type of assignment before, and the output gave me practical and usable ideas about how to write the assignment guidelines and how to make the assignment potentially more engaging for students. The outputs definitely effectively addressed the needs I expressed in my prompts.  The only shortcomings, I think, lie in some of the wording I used for the prompts, which could have been more precise in some cases, and aren’t really due to the output itself.  I also should have stipulated that students should be instructed to put some actual quotations from the text within their responses, so I’ll need to give more consideration to my actual expectations for the assignment.  With more experimentation and practice in prompt engineering, I believe I could improve upon the outputs, but they’ve already been pretty productive.  The ideas these outputs have given me already will be a great help as I’m brainstorming this particular assignment, trying to make it more engaging, and deciding upon my grading criteria. Definitely I’m convinced about UT Verses’ value in producing ideas for creating and improving course assignments and the criteria for grading them.

I have written this response twice now and tried submitting this discussion post and get an error message each time that there is a problem submitting the post. My other response was a bit longer than this. 
I do think the ideas generated by the AI are helpful. The suggestions are effective, but need revision. This might be addressed with continued revising of my prompts. The AI does not provide much in the way of real world examples in this prompt that seem more fleshed out. The responses are correct, but academic. I do like that the AI can respond in any tone I ask it to--that is really interesting and fun. In my courses, I will be asking students to use AI to help generate their own work and solidify their own findings and ideas. I think this is the most effective approach for my own purposes and it will help teach students about prompt engineering and how to use the AI to enhance their learning.

I thought the response was really good! I was surprised that the response was basically an entire lesson plan for introducing students to the topic. I might actually use this for the coming semester.

I thought the response was relevant to the course, the content, and was beyond the expectation that I had.

I think this is a good basic outline for a lesson and discussion.

As I am in the midst of creating my Fall course syllabus, I wanted to see whether I could harness The UT Verse. Among the four choices, I thought its localized knowledge was most likely to yield the 'best' response.

The response did not contain the specificity that I was seeking because it does not possess real-time data. Instead, I was given a generic decision-rule that I already follow to make the determination.

I am happy that I posed this prompt because I was interested in exploring the 'no real time data' limitation.
with the revisions I made to the prompt, I thought this was a very insightful exercise.

Overall, I found it very interesting that I was provided with a rationale for each of the 10 assessment suggestions, as well as an overall justification for the list.  Although I did not agree with all of the options for feasibility and other reasons, the rationale actually provided me with additional insight on other potential types of assessments.  In essence, I liked the rationale/justification although I disagreed with the assessment.

I liked the professional development plan and online discussion forum ideas.  I find I do not use the discussion forums much in my courses, probably because I am not sure how students may benefit from them. But seeing this example made a lot of sense and I could see more clearly how this discussion forum exercise could spur creative thoughts about each students own approach to professional development.  Very nice.  I also like the professional development plan as this allows the student to self determine and take responsibility to a plan. It may not be exactly what they do, but at least they have already had the opportunity to think about professional goals, their business context, and what might be an approach/plan for professional development. 

As tis is one of those higher order outcomes, I like that the options are tangible and provide me with good ideas to implement that get at the assessment of this type of outcome.  And it seems more engaging than other approaches I have thought through on my own.

I revised my outcomes and used chatgpt . All the activities that came out are very similar to the ones I will be using, Nothing really stands out as "wow I didn't think about it!" so not very thrilled with the outcome, but I do have quite few interesting activities so it is ok.

I really do like the ideas that Gemini generated for me. I would like to spend some time thinking deeper about how I can set my students up to utilize digital tools for a volunteer recruitment campaign. I already have them complete a similar assignment, but this takes it up a notch. I think the generated ideas align well to effectively access my SLOs. I think for me some of the shortcomings or initial concerns I notice are that this course is a distance taught, asynchronous online course so my touch points with the students looks different than if we were F2F or even Knoxville campus, asynchronous.

This is a pretty straightfortward breakdown of assignments and activities that are often used in teaching creative writing, whether in person, in synchronous online courses, or asynchronous online courses. I have always (in person) used portfolio assignments that include creative work accompanied by reflective essays that ask students to explain their process and articulate the craft goals they were trying to meet; I have also always used written peer reviews as a way to build students' vocabulary of craft and revision tactics.  

There isn't much here that seems especially tailored to the asynchronous online format--the apps mentioned in the very last bullet point (Tiki-Toki and Sutori) are ones I hadn't heard of and might be useful, especially if they encourage students to work collaboratively. My in-person classes generate (by design) a strong sense of community and camaraderie among students, and I am still looking for ways to cultivate those values in an asynchronous class. Specifically, I am still looking for more nuanced ideas about how to encourage students to truly connect in asynchronous online discussions.

This was a good exercise for me!  I used the app and I do use many of these assessments in my courses
I see several assignments that I already use which confirmed my choice of assessments. I love the fact that it tells you which learning outcome is being measured with each assessment. That will actually help me when I do my class revisions to make sure I am measuring what I think I am measuring with my assessments.

I use 7, 8, 9, and 10 in this course. I also teach a communications research course with similar outcomes and therefore similar assessments. In that course I use assessments 4, 5, and 6 

the first three ideas feel more like undergraduate level assessments or too theory based for me.

I think the ideas generated above by generative AI provide a useful starting point when building assessments that align with learning outcomes! I find I would rather "revise" than to "create," so the AI output allows for me to think critically and act as the subject matter expert when considering how to build appropriate assessments. These ideas do a relatively good job offering diverse assessment possibilities, but do not span Bloom's Taxonomy quite as well.
I think the following recommendation makes sense to incorporate as an assessment:
"Assign students to draft a report recommending a financial course of action for a sports franchise or entertainment company, supported by data analysis."
I like this assessment as it is a culminating, applied assessment integrating the scaffolding elements of various financial calculations, interpretations, and case studies. One modification I could make is to provide a sample report -- and/or excerpt of a report -- and ask them to reflect on the recommendations provided prior to constructing their own report.

I leveraged UT Verse for this exercise and found the use of AI to be a helpful thought-starter for my course. That said, I identified challenges with practical implementation.

 

On a positive note, case study analyses and discussion forums were suggested, which I found helpful and validating. Discussion forums are commonplace in online asynchronous education so this feedback was validating. I would not have considered a case study analysis for this particular course, so my wheels are turning. Additionally, UT Verse suggested simulation games, which I would not have otherwise considered. 

 

As a response to the prompt, UT Verse suggested role playing exercises, which are commonplace in a synchronous learning environment; however, impractical in an asynchronous environment. Additionally, UT Verse suggested guest interviews which are less likely to occur in an asynchronous environment; however, it can be adapted to a podcast or pre-recorded experience. On the whole, I found the use of AI helpful and a time-saver; however, it requires adaptation before simply copy/pasting into curriculum.

I do think the ideas are very useful. I got a lot of great answers in less than 10 seconds. I cannot do all of the suggestions due to time constraints, and that learning outcome just being a small portion of the class, but it gave me great ideas for other assignments and courses as well.

One of the suggestions was to develop a fake budget for a theoretical project (which they do). I plan to still incorporate that. But what I did like was the reflective journaling. I think instead of a reflection at the end of the course, they could turn in a journal documenting their learning experiences, challenges, and insights throughout the course with each assignment. I also did like the online discussion forum. We have a discussion board for each module, but I like the prompts they suggested. I feel this would be very helpful when creating a course from scratch.

AI has generated a great summary of the different methods that can be used for the learning activity practices. Personally, I like the following ideas: set up discussion boards where students engage in asynchronous conversations,  involve students into creating the course materials, real-time feedback through polls that can be used to adjust a course setup. I believe if I incorporate these ideas in my course properly, they will be very effective in engaging students to discuss the course material and as consequence develop critical and analytical thinking.

I can see a couple of shortcomings in this list: 1) many ideas are repeated from topic to topic; 2) most of ideas are very general and you need to do additional search to find details of their use in practice.

I don't feel like the activities that Chat GPT gave me were necessarily helpful as those would be things I would do anyway as part of student notes and group work.  However, a nice reminder was added at the end to include a mix of both formative and summative assessments as well as to consider incorporating collaborative activities, discussions, and feedback sessions.  I think in an asynchronous environment it is easy to forget that these things are still just as important as they are in a synchronous course and that I should be mindful to also incorporate them in an asynchronous course.






The UT Verse Chat was a little more creative.  It gave a few better ideas regarding using Desmos to have students explore discontinuities of functions and using project based assignments for assessment of the real world applications:

For this assignment I included only the response I got from ChatGPT 3.5 (which I used twice) but I also checked out Bing Chat, Poe, and Google Bard. I am not at all a frequent user of AI (in the way I used it here) and I was pleasantly surprised. Several of the suggestions I got from Bing Chat were assessments and activities I already use. So, that was an amazing validation for me! I choose to present here the ideas I got from ChatGPT 3.5 because they were more creative and several were “things” I wouldn’t have thought about!

I believe for my classes I will be using a combination of the ideas I got from the different Chatbots I accessed! I LOVED this assignment!

I think a lot of the idea the AI gave are very good.  I like the learning activity for my fifth learning outcome to involve more virtual field trips. In the igneous rock module, I go over the volcanic eruption of Mt. St. Helens and for the sedimentary rock module, we take a virtual field trip to the Grand Canyon but I think it would be useful to apply more examples with different modules too. One of the shortcomings based off the AI generated ideas is the lack of field experience. Field experience is crucial for the field of geology and is easier to implement than you think; not discounting virtual field trip, those are very good too as they use world examples. For example, I have had students look at the rock garden for rock examples or had them walk around campus (or wherever they are) to become more aware of their environmental surroundings. I think it helps students understand that everything in the book does not just stay in the book and that the processes we study are currently happening outside your front door!

I think the one of the best recommendations that came out of the AI generation is on my 3 learning objective about defining and applying relevant terminology in Earth sciences by having the students develop a glossary for terms. This could be done through a discussion board and then the final results be compiled into a document so the students could use them to study. With enough students in the class, each study could be in charge of 1-2 terms to define for the whole class.

Some of the AI-generated ideas might be useful. The ideas that strike me as most promising are the Argument Analysis Assignments, the Descartes Objection Response Paper, and the Quizzes and Assessments. I think that items from these categories could be used to facilitate and/or gauge progress toward learning outcomes. Discussion Forums might also have some value relative to my learning outcomes, but I think that very detailed instructions and tight monitoring/feedback would be needed. I am less sure of the value and/or feasibility of the Research Paper and Interactive Online suggestions. The first suggestion under Quizzes and Assessments strikes me as an especially good way to test for ability to explain and use fundamental concepts of argument extraction and evaluation.

The AI's response makes me wonder what information it crawled to come up with its answers. I already apply all of the methodologies and assessment models that ChatGPT suggested in my Workplace Writing Online course. I am not sure whether that means I am on the right track and have developed a well-constructed asynchronous online course, or if it means that the AI located data that I have unknowingly contributed to given my course has been online since 2017. It has gone through multiple revisions through the years and I know I did not invent the wheel, but the similarities to what the AI arrived at and what I am already doing in my course are striking. This also leads me to wonder about the prompt itself; could my prompt have been enough information for the AI to return a result suggesting assessment and activity models that are so much like the course I already teach? I am not sure whether I will use ChatGPT in the future for course development; however, since I teach workplace writing and public writing, I have devised some assignments for my students that prompt them to use AI. Interestingly, ChatGPT did not reference itself in its recommendations--it did not suggest using AI to generate an assignment or assessment model. Perhaps that would be a bit too "meta" for the AI.

ChatGPT offers a litany of assignments commonly found our seminar courses. While there is nothing particularly novel in the suggestions themselves, I do appreciate and have gained from the way ChatGPT's responses help articulate how such old standbys specifically address learning outcomes.
One suggestion I found especially useful was the self-assessment/self-critical reflection assignment (#11).  While I have in the past used a form of critical reflection in the form of an online journal in some seminars, I have always been somewhat at a loss as to how to assess the results. Even when layered so that students go back to reflect on their past reflections, I am not sure how to (or if one even can) assess another's progress in critical reflection using grading criteria...other than good faith effort and being on time. ChatGPT has not helped me here. My sense has been that self-reflective assignments have been some of the most productive and impactful assignments for my students. But I find assessment to be a real enigma. 

Hopefully I can learn from folks here, as I would like to include more kinds of self-reflection assignments into my course(s)

Chatgpt has been a valuable asset that I've used this semester to create in-class activities for the class I am currently assisting with. I find that all of the suggestions are doable; however, the recommendations are very writing intensive, which may not be the best solution for an intro-level class. I would use some of the suggestions, such as the concept mapping and presentation for my class, and file away the other ideas for later use. In general Chatgpt has always given me a good base to plan my activities, and I will continue to use it to aid me in planning my classes.

I have never used ChatGPT before, and now that I have, I'm petrified by the results.  I use just about all these suggestions in my course.  So not only are the suggestions helpful, but they solidify what I'm already doing.  Two suggestions, however, that I do not currently employ but could be quite useful are the Case Study Audience Analysis and the Audience Engagement Analytics.  Too many times, the semester ends with students acknowledging they wish they had chosen a different audience for their crowdfunding campaign.  I already have them brainstorm and justify their audience choice before creating their campaign, but having a more structured, researched assignment might be helpful.  Additionally, I have students who are very critical of social media posts in our analysis stage but then commit the same flaws in their application.  So doing an analysis of how engaging those social media posts are for their target audience might help them better reflect on what they did well and/or what they could do better.

I am familiar with this AI tool to create activities for my course. I liked the ideas, but all are not doable for a semester-long course.  I am already using some of them.  I like that the ideas generated emphasize communicative activities; students convey new information to their peers and instructors, meaning they create with the language. And using Bloom's Taxonomy, students create with the language to produce new and original work.  All activities require interaction, which reflects that AI understands language acquisition as communication per se.

I am surprised at how well Bard did with generating activities that respond to my four proposed learning outcomes. Interesting enough, these are assignments that are part of the standard UTK first-year composition curriculum. While some of the activities use generic description that are often repetitive of what my outcomes are (as expected with AI?), I would elaborate on the activity prompt and fit them into my course syllabus.

The first activity would be a good thing to incorporate into my course. Google Bard proposes: “Activity: Have students create a presentation or blog post in which they compare and contrast two different texts on the same topic, analyzing the different ways in which the writers approach the topic and persuade their readers.” We currently have that in the curriculum, called the Comparative Rhetorical Analysis (CRA) but it takes the form of a more formal academic paper. It would be interesting to propose it in the context of a presentation of blog post, because it ties in with the fourth learning outcome of using social, cultural, and historical contexts to compare the texts. Instead of just comparing the two texts, a student would also get to apply the content in a different way, centered on new information and their own interpretation. This hits the “apply” stage of Bloom’s Taxonomy.

The activities suggested by AI are very effective. I am going to incorporate suggestions such as quizzes, case studies, lab reports, group projects, etc. However, considering this is a 100 level course, research papers or experimental design projects suggested by AI are more suitable for higher level courses.


Interestingly, most of these suggested activities are in use during current teaching activities.

I used UT Verse for today's assignment. Before working with the AI program, I edited my learning objectives after receiving peer and instructor feedback.

 

Overall, I'm impressed with UT Verse's ability to quickly generate assignments that closely connect with what I want students to get out of my course. Some AI ideas, like inviting guest speakers and assigning a museum exhibit critique, are almost exactly what I'd like to incorporate in my final syllabus. I really want to challenge students' perceptions of history, as just sitting and reading books. These particular AI ideas could help me show students that doing history is so much more. It involves interacting with, critiquing, growing conversations about, and revising historical arguments, people, moments, places, and things.

 

Other AI ideas, like the assignment of both short response essays and long research papers, are pretty standard in my field. Because of this, I was sadly disappointed by the first assignment which addresses the overall theme of my course: UT Verse suggested that I assign a research paper or project for students to explore how love, devotion, and other intimate feelings have shaped history. I was underwhelmed by this response, as, again, this is standard practice. But this does not reflect a problem with AI. Secondly, it is unrealistic to expect students--in a large survey course--to "write expansively" in this way. That being said, I'd adjust this objective so that survey courses write concisely, while upper-division courses write expansively.

 

Moving forward, I could see myself using an AI generator, like UT Verse, as a starting point for my "big picture" assignments on the syllabus. I recognize that in order to more effectively use AI, I have to edit my objectives down--which includes changing or omitting too much language on writing, if that is my goal. So, this assignment definitely made me realize how much emphasis I had on writing in my learning objectives! Either way, this was a great learning experience (this was my first time ever using AI)!

ChatGPT responded with several good ideas for accompanying the outcomes I provided. Similar to what a couple of others noted, a few of the suggestions made are difficult to implement in an asynchronous course (debates, for example). The suggestion for interviews is particularly difficult regardless of the modality of the course, as those involved in judicial process are usually difficult to reach for interviews (this type of outcome is reasonable for other professionals, though). I am a huge fan of the suggested case analysis papers and interactive infographics/mind maps.

Currently, I use a mock Supreme Court simulation to model theories of decision making. For this activity, students take on roles of interest groups, attorneys, and justices to go through the decision making process from certiorari through opinion writing. It is a great experience for my students (in their own words), and using case analysis papers to introduce or conclude the activity could be useful. The idea of using mind maps to distinguish differences between state and federal courts could be useful as well, as the terminology used in the different legal systems can be confusing, as well as the structures of state judiciaries versus the federal judiciary.

Overall, I think generative AI did an ok job at suggesting activities to accompany the inputted learning outcomes. However, the shortcomings I expressed regarding matching assignments to the modality of the course and ensuring activities are appropriate and achievable are likely to be common and thus we should be cautiously mindful of them. Good as a supplement or inspiration.

To be honest, I am not quite sure how helpful the generative AI was for helping me to identify assessments and learning activities for my course.  The ideas generated to address my learning outcomes seemed to struggle with the fact that my subfield of study tends be very jargon laden and has a very specific way of phrasing things.  I recognize that my CLOs are a bit ambitious and maybe less complex than ideal, but the textbook I like to use is written in a way to support these learning objectives.  Since this is my second textbook I have used for this course, I actually find this approach very useful from a social science perspective, so I am struggling to deviate away from it because of its unique approach.

I think generative AI is a great way of potentially being inspired but I do not find it any more helpful than simply meeting with other scholars in my subfield who enjoy engaging in pedagogy and want to find new, innovative, and meaningful ways of teaching the subject matter.  I will say that it may better help me organize how I think by providing a framework to start with.  But it feels a bit like Googling a topic – you need to be able to critically assess the value of the output before simply feeling like it’s the answer to your prayers.

I think the case study analysis, theory and history synthesis, levels of analysis application, perspective analysis, and simulations are all valuable in an online course.  I will say that I may not do separately, but rather incorporate aspects of each across different assignments, which may muddle things without clear rubrics and course mapping.  I love simulations and actually would like to find a better way to incorporate them in an online asynchronous course.  My students have taken more away from simulations than I expected and tend to be able to make the connections I like them to see when they get the chance to do them and when I properly prepare them.

This was my first time using AI to generate ideas for assessments and learning activities, and I was pleasantly surprised at the quality of the responses. The AI appears to be paying close attention to the verbs and "cognitive processes" in the learning outcomes, and matching these to appropriate types of activities (as we have been encouraged to do using Bloom's taxonomy, etc. as our guides). For instance, LO #2 uses "Discuss", and the activities suggested were a discussion forum and an essay-type written response listing the strengths and weaknesses of different plot types.

That said, some of the suggestions were fairly generic. A written "essay" was suggested for the "Explain" outcome in LO#5; a more appropriate assessment for this introductory level may be to have students succinctly explain the idea with reference to a sketch or a plot. For the "Distinguish between" outcome (LO #3), ChatGPT suggested an activity classifying distributions as "discrete or continuous". This is a potential start, but we also want to know that students understand the conceptual differences between these types of distributions (e.g. why we use probability mass functions to define discrete probability distributions, and probability density functions to define continuous probability distributions). Overall, these results are a good starting point, but instructors may need to add to these activities or modify them to suit their audience and increase their relevance.

 

We already use some of the inquiry-based learning approaches suggested by ChatGPT: we rely heavily on R-coding assignments and provide research scenarios and "real data" for students to analyze in R, both in demos/ tutorials and in formative/ summative assessments. We prompt students to reflect on their approaches.

An idea here that we do not typically use that I would consider incorporating into a course: using discussion forums. Posting to a discussion forum could be a great pre-class exercise to prime students to think about the topics that will be presented in a video tutorial or Zoom lecture, for e.g. how to present data in an effective manner. Before class, students could review a document that presents the same data using different types of plots and have to reflect on which figures do a good job of communicating the main idea, and whether any of the plots may bias our interpretations.

I think these suggestions are useful and respond to the outcomes very well. Its response/suggestions for how to evaluate the outcome of engaging Spanish Speaking Communities is something that we do in the course already. It suggested for the students to join an online community and participate in discussions and they do that through EnVivo (Lingua Meeting) in which each student has a weekly meeting with a native Spanish speaker, and they talk for 30 minutes catered to the grammar and vocab that is being covered that week. Had this not already been part of the course, I would have most certainly tried to include the AI suggestion in some way, shape, or form.

The most useful activities it came up with were in response to "Analyze and Evaluate Perspectives and Practices of Hispanic Cultures" and "Cultures (Products, Practices, Perspectives)" in which it proposed to introduce the students to Spanish based literature and other content, and have them reflect and analyze (tailored to the desired level); for the culture, the AI suggested to show movies, music, or news. This is related to the analyzing outcome, but different enough that both could be included.

I have used AI several times in creating lesson plans for my SPAN 111 course, and I ask it to help me come up with input or output based activities that deal with the content of the lesson

I was very surprised by the AI response that I got. Some of these assignments I have actually used a version of in an online course and an in person course on American Literature to 1865, and I've even found that those assignments work well and the students enjoy them! I did find this activity to be useful overall. The ideas that were generated by AI would be helpful to me as they align with my learning outcomes and offer a great starting point to think of assessments for this course. As for effectiveness, I do think that the ideas are effective for the most part. The ideas were writing centered, which I think go well with a literature course. I wouldn't use all of the writing ideas because I don't think all of them align with my overall goal for the course. Some of the written assessments would be more beneficial than others, like the textual analysis essay, comparative essay, or the literary movement research paper. Some of these assessments I have done, but I haven't tried the literary movement paper. That could be neat to explore! I also think that the generated assessments lend themselves well to helping students emphasize the literature of the period, forming connections and understanding the importance of the texts.

As for shortcomings, there were a few assignments that I either wouldn't use or don't think are effective for a literature course. The assessment that I immediately wrote off was peer review. I don't think a literature course needs to incorporate peer review as it doesn't align with any of the learning outcomes. I did think there were too many writing assignments included as well. I want the emphasis to be on reading, with the written assignments helping the students understand the literature even more. I don't mind presentations, discussion boards, or even the reading journal, but I wouldn't want them to write more than 3-4 papers over the course of the class. Other than that, I would have liked to have seen more assessments focusing on stronger reading comprehension or assessments aligning with reading more than writing.

There were some assessments that I could effectively use in an online course for American Literature to 1865.  The critical reading journals would allow students to focus on the material within the readings, while also recording their thoughts in writing. These journals would help with comprehension, but they could also be a good resource to look back on for papers. This could be a course long assignment. Online discussions would be useful as well because they can help students track certain literary movements, themes, or connections within the texts we would read. Discussion boards could be implemented across the semester as well, especially as needed or for important concepts the students need to learn. I like having at least one presentation in a literature course, so the thematic analysis project could be useful for this. Students would pick one theme, author, or literary movement to present on. I think this would work best in the middle or the end of the course. As for papers that I could assign throughout the semester, I like the textual analysis paper, which could be assigned up to two times in the course; the literary mode analysis, which would be assigned once; and either the comparative essay or the literary timeline, which would also be assigned just once. These assignments would be longer papers that students would work on throughout the course to emphasize the readings.

I believe that this is a very useful too. Not only do I believe that some of the suggested assessments are useful, but a few are ones that I already use in my class that has these outcomes. I believe that I expect students to be able to demonstrate in some way their knowledge of key concepts regarding social inequality. I also expect them to be able to connect these concepts to their real lived lives. I believe assessments like a final term paper and discussion forums where I ask them to do these specific tasks are useful. I would say the only problem with the suggestions of actual assessments are the lack of variety. I believe there are more creative assessments out there, but Bard only lists it as a possibility.

I already use a midterm exam in which I created a profile of someone (a vignette essentially) and asked them to relate the inequalities this person would have faced in particular institutions and settings. This acts them to locate specific examples within larger social contexts. I also use the discussion forums, because it allows them to interact with each other and use each other as resources to reach a broader understanding.

This is not my first time using generative AI to get teaching inspirations. Overall, the ideas are useful. As some peers have commented, some ideas suggested are already incorporated in the class. AI might not generate any truly original idea, but at least gives us some examples of what can be incorporated if we haven't already. The assessment parts of the response address the learning objectives effectively. Some of the descriptions are generic, as other peers have pointed out, but it is not always possible to get the most tailored response on the first go.

I asked ChatGPT to elaborate more on number 4, and borrowed some of the ideas. I would love to have an online class activity where students ask the online graphing calculator to graph a list of provided functions, and find a list of function values by both looking at the graph and by computing algebraically, and then compare the answers. This can be a group project, with peer review as part of the assessment.

Overall, these ideas match up very well with my learning outcomes.  I was surprised at the similarities between what I currently do in my in-person course and the ideas generated by ChapGPT. 

Several learning activities that ChatGPT suggested, I already use in the form of out-of-class practice problems.  With enough time and planning, I think these could easily be adapted into an online environment – maybe in the form of Canvas quizzes or discussion boards.  I found the case study suggestion to be particularly interesting.  I have several case studies that I have collected over the years.  It is difficult to carve out time to go over those during an in-person course.  I think these could work very well for an online course!

Other suggestions from ChatGPT have shortcomings.  A group synthesis project would be especially challenging for students taking a 200-level course; however, it could be a fun and challenging idea to implement for students taking a 300- or 400-level chemistry course.  I also like the idea of peer review for problems.  One of the best ways to learn organic chemistry is to learn from your mistakes (and the mistakes of others).  However, my main concern is that struggling students will not be able to provide meaningful feedback to their peers.

I thought ChatGPT’s responses to the general learning outcomes and situating further assessment and learning activities were relatively on point. To be honest, a lot of the responses were in line with what UTK’s English program and I already make an effort to do in class. With respect to Learning Outcome #3, though, which is to have students write persuasive arguments that articulate a clear, thoughtful position, I was intrigued by the potential of having students engage with International Case Studies, where students situate for foreign matter/perspective and articulate it to an audience unfamiliar with the topic or geographic area.

Insofar as I thought this was a productive exercise, the activity asks that students not only engage with the premise of writing on a specific position, it broadens their cultural perspectives by having them engage with a culture other than their own.

In my mind, this international case study offers a dynamic way for students to engage with the world around them and think about their relationality to global citizenry, challenging them to think about community writ large. I see myself engaging with this exercise in my own class by potentially starting the global report off on a smaller scale, requesting students respond to a weekly discussion post or the like. Here, I would ask students to find two to three source reporting on their given issues and then write a five-minute script of their own where they explore, via video response, introducing the issue to their peers. Afterwards, this project might be scaled to encompass a broader perspective, which is incorporated into a larger semester project.

I like the idea of majority of these suggestions, including having multiple quizzes and exams.  Regular quizzes are great at assessing knowledge of microbial cell structure and function. Midterm and final exams covering microbial metabolism and gene expression principles are also a very good assessment tool to assess understanding of concepts among a wide range of students with variation in retaining power  of data and basic concepts. Discussion threads where students discuss recent scientific articles related to microbial cell structure and its functional components, encourage critical analysis and application of concepts.

Case Studies and research presentations :  Real-life case studies involving microbial cell structures, medically relation of microbes , including bacteria and viruses in infections and immunity help  students to analyze and propose solutions based on their understanding. Interactive Simulations are also a good tool to use in microbiology :Provide links to interactive simulations where students can virtually explore microbial cell structures and functions. These all can be implemented in my course.

 

Virtual Labs are the shortcomings in microbiology courses especially trying to incorporate aseptic techniques virtually is a not so successful as I have seen during COVID times trying to teach infection and immunity online.

I am a little skeptical of large language models, but I am definitely impressed by UT Verse's performance here. While there is a lot of repetition of the initial prompt, the learning activities line up fairly well with those that I already have in mind. The activities are generally presented with a bird's-eye view, but I was sold on the additional details about reviewing Git histories and the inclusion of the matplotlib plotting library for Python as an example of an in-language plotting tool. I also enjoyed the depth demonstrated in the sentence about emphasizing good written communication skills. 

I believe that all of these suggestions could be implemented in my course, but I am particularly fond of the suggestion about using Git with a sample project, and believe that this could be implemented by giving students a "broken" project that is fixed through a sequence of exercises, with those fixes then individually pushed and committed to a personal remote repository on GitHub.

Many of these suggestions are recycled from popular varieties of writing exercises, several that I already use in the classroom, including the mock debate and video analysis for appeals. Some of the other suggestions are a vague. Like much of the material derived from AI generated models, some is useful, but much is unhelpful. Somewhat useful for generating ideas around classrooms assignments.

I believe that Google Bard provided many useful ideas for my course design.  I love their suggestions of how to incorporate an online discussion forum and also their ideas for the research paper(s).  However, there are a few shortcomings with these ideas as well.  For example, it would be rather difficult to implement any sort of group debate element, as the class I am designing will meet exclusively asynchronously.  Even more, I love the idea of a historical reenactment of American Politics, but this would likely prove even more difficult than the group debate in an online setting.  Though the previous two ideas will likely not be incorporated into my course, I do believe that the personal reflection component will take a front seat for me.  I believe that self-reflection is an important part of learning, and I am happy to see that Google Bard has provided me with a personal reflection option in their learning outcomes.  I will likely take this idea and run with it even more through reflection journal assignments.  These assignments could be due each week based on the topics we are discussing in class at the time.  All in all, this exercise gave me some great ideas for the future.

The ideas generated align effectively with the specified learning outcomes, providing a range of assessment and learning activities that directly target each outcome. The activities suggested by Chat GPT are not necessarily novel; however, they are accurate. They also promote active engagement with the material and provide opportunities for students to practice their understanding of the learning outcomes.The idea of incorporating Discussion Forums into classes can be effectively implemented, as these are in an asynchronous format, allowing students to use the language for communication while maintaining their learning pace

Implementing everything listed here will be great but time factor in a semester long course will be an hindrance. I am particularly fascinated by the use of examples that can be modeled into real applications, that will increase participation and enable better understanding of the subject matter. Also, timely feedback to gauge progress and understanding is also crucial for direction.
